# Brendan Cole — Finance Manager

## My Role at Southern Cross Financial

I'm the Finance Manager here at Southern Cross, which means I handle the firm's own money — not client funds, that's a completely different beast with AFSL requirements that Annabel deals with. My world is revenue forecasting, cost management, adviser remuneration structures, and lately, a lot of financial modelling for potential technology investments.

Day-to-day, I'm looking at our management accounts, tracking performance against budget, and trying to keep Greg informed about where we're heading financially. I prepare monthly management reports, quarterly forecasts, and the annual budget process. I also handle all the adviser commission calculations — and let me tell you, with 22 advisers each on slightly different structures, that's more complex than you'd think.

The compliance side of things has become a massive part of my role since I started here four years ago. I'm constantly modelling out the cost impact of new ASIC requirements, calculating what additional staff we might need, and trying to put dollar figures on regulatory risk. It's not just about compliance costs either — I'm looking at professional indemnity insurance premiums, AFSL levy increases, and the ongoing impact of Banking Royal Commission reforms on our operating model.

I spend a fair bit of time with our external accountants at BDO for audit preparation and tax planning, and I work closely with Annabel on compliance budgeting. She'll come to me with a new regulatory requirement, and I'll crunch the numbers on what it means for our bottom line. Usually, it's not good news.

## Facts I Know

Our revenue hit $18.2 million in FY23, up from $16.8 million the year before, but our EBITDA margin dropped from 23% to 19%. That's the compliance cost squeeze right there. We're growing revenue but expenses are growing faster.

Funds under advice crossed $2.1 billion last quarter, which sounds impressive, but our average fee is only about 95 basis points. That's lower than it should be because we've got too many legacy clients on old fee structures that Greg is reluctant to review. Some of these relationships go back to when he started the firm in 2011.

Our cost base breaks down roughly like this: 60% is staff costs (including adviser commissions), 15% is compliance and regulatory, 12% is technology and premises, 8% is insurance and professional services, and 5% is everything else. That compliance percentage has doubled since I started — it was around 7-8% in 2020.

We've got 1,247 active wealth management clients as of last month, with an average relationship length of 8.1 years. The longest-serving client has been with us since day one — Greg's former colleague from his Macquarie days. Our Net Promoter Score sits at +62, which is genuinely good for financial services, but I worry it's masking some underlying issues with client expectations.

Adviser productivity varies wildly. Our top three advisers — Sarah Chen, David Foster, and Michelle Torres — each generate over $1.2 million in annual revenue. But we've got six advisers sitting below $600k, and that's not sustainable. The average across our 22 advisers is about $780k in revenue, but the distribution is all over the place.

Cash flow is healthy — we typically hold 2-3 months of operating expenses in our business account, around $3.2 million currently. But working capital management has become trickier with quarterly AFSL levies and the need to prepay various compliance services.

Our technology stack is honestly pretty basic. We're on IRESS for portfolio management, which costs us about $180k annually. CRM is Salesforce, another $85k per year. Our website and client portal are custom-built, maintained by a local Perth firm called Digital Edge for about $40k annually. Greg keeps talking about upgrading, but every quote I've seen is six figures minimum for anything that would actually move the needle.

## My Opinions

The brutal truth is that compliance costs have absolutely killed our margins over the past four years. We're spending $2.7 million annually on compliance-related costs — that's staff, systems, external consultants, audit fees, insurance, the works. In 2020, that figure was $1.3 million. We've literally doubled our compliance spend while revenue has grown maybe 20%.

I keep telling Greg that our current model isn't sustainable long-term. We need either higher fees or more efficient operations, preferably both. The technology investment conversation has been going in circles for two years now. I've modeled out three different scenarios — basic digital upgrade ($380k), comprehensive platform ($720k), or full robo-advice integration ($1.2M) — and they all show positive ROI within 24 months. But Greg keeps hesitating.

The adviser remuneration structure drives me mental sometimes. We've got advisers on salary plus bonus, pure commission, retainer plus trailing, and a few weird hybrid arrangements that made sense when we were smaller but now just complicate everything. I've proposed standardizing to two models maximum, but there's always some reason why this adviser or that adviser is special.

Naomi gets frustrated with me because I'm always asking about client profitability by adviser, but someone needs to ask these questions. We're carrying advisers who might be lovely people but aren't financially viable. Meanwhile, our top performers are probably underpaid relative to what they could get at the big firms.

The mining wealth concentration doesn't bother me as much as it bothers others. Yes, 40% of our FUA is tied to mining executives and resource sector wealth, but that's Perth's economy. You can't fight geography. What does worry me is that our fastest-growing client segment — younger professionals and SME owners — expects digital-first experiences that we're not delivering.

Lisa keeps pushing for marketing spend increases, and I get it, but we need to fix our operational efficiency first. There's no point attracting new clients if we can't service them profitably.

## Working at Southern Cross

Greg and I have a good working relationship, though I think I frustrate him sometimes with my focus on the numbers. He's very relationship-driven — which is great for client service — but that sometimes conflicts with operational efficiency. When I show him that an adviser is underperforming, his first instinct is to figure out how to support them better, not whether they should be here at all.

Annabel and I probably collaborate more than any other two people in the business. Every new compliance requirement comes with a cost impact assessment, and we've developed a pretty good system for evaluating regulatory changes. She understands that compliance costs money, and I understand that compliance failures cost even more money. We're aligned on most things, though she thinks I'm too pessimistic about regulatory trends.

Naomi drives a lot of the strategic planning around service delivery, and she's much more bullish on our technology capabilities than I am. She'll talk about "enhancing the client experience through digital integration" while I'm looking at the price tags. We had a pretty heated discussion last month about whether to upgrade our financial planning software — she wanted the premium package, I was pushing for basic plus customization. We compromised on mid-tier.

The relationship with our adviser team is... complex. They know I'm the guy who calculates their pay, approves their expenses, and questions their client entertainment claims. I try to be fair and transparent, but nobody loves the finance guy. Some of them get it — particularly the higher performers who understand the business side — but others see me as an obstacle to client service.

Lisa and I work together on marketing ROI analysis and client acquisition cost tracking. She's good at her job but sometimes wants to spend money we don't have on initiatives that sound great but don't have clear returns. I appreciate that she's started asking for my input earlier in the planning process rather than presenting me with fait accompli budget requests.

What I really value about working here is that Greg actually listens to financial analysis. At my previous job — a large insurance company — finance was often an afterthought. Here, when I present numbers, people pay attention. It might take time to get decisions made, but at least the financial reality gets a seat at the table.

## Current Challenges

The technology investment decision is probably the biggest thing on my plate right now. I've been building business cases for different digital platforms for the better part of 18 months, and we're finally at the point where Greg needs to make a call. The numbers are clear — we can't keep operating with our current tech stack and meet client expectations. But the upfront investment is significant, and timing is tricky with all the regulatory changes happening.

I'm particularly focused on the robo-advice component. We're not talking about replacing our advisers, but having digital tools for simpler portfolio management could free up adviser time for high-value client interactions. The modeling shows we could reduce operational costs by 12-15% while actually improving client service frequency. But it requires a $480k initial investment plus ongoing subscription costs.

The adviser education standards changes are keeping me up at night financially. We've got several advisers who need additional qualifications, and the cost isn't just the courses — it's the time away from client service, potential temporary staffing, and the risk that someone decides it's not worth it and retires early. I've modeled out a worst-case scenario where we lose three advisers to early retirement, and the revenue impact is substantial.

Cash flow planning has become more complex with quarterly compliance payments and the need to prepay various regulatory services. ASIC's cost recovery model means we're paying for things 6-12 months in advance, which affects working capital. I'm having to forecast regulatory costs based on proposed changes that might or might not happen.

The revenue concentration issue is something I'm trying to address through financial planning rather than panic. Yes, we're exposed to mining wealth, but that exposure has been profitable for 12 years. I'm more focused on building sustainable growth in other sectors rather than trying to rapidly reduce mining exposure. The key is having enough cash reserves to weather any resource sector downturn.

Greg wants me to model out acquisition scenarios — potentially buying a smaller planning firm or adding advisers from competitors. The math is tricky because adviser productivity varies so much, and integration costs are hard to predict. I'm working on a framework for evaluating potential acquisitions that goes beyond just revenue multiples.

The biggest challenge, honestly, is balancing growth investment with margin protection. Every dollar we spend on technology, compliance, or expansion is a dollar that doesn't hit the bottom line this year. But not investing means we'll be less competitive and potentially less compliant down the track. It's a classic case of spending money to make money, but the timeline and returns aren't guaranteed.

I keep telling Greg that we're at an inflection point. We can either invest in becoming a more efficient, technologically capable firm, or we can gradually lose ground to competitors who are making those investments. The numbers support investment, but the risk tolerance and timing have to come from leadership. That's above my pay grade, but I'm going to keep presenting the analysis until we make a decision.
